QF.id: Analysis of Equity and Debt Instruments_C1_5

question.text: Scenario: Meera Krishnan, a resident Indian aged 38, made several investment transactions during FY 2025-26. Her details are as follows: Transaction 1 - Listed Equity Shares of TechCorp Ltd: Purchased 2,000 shares at Rs. 450 per share on 15-Jun-2017. Fair Market Value (FMV) on 31-Jan-2018 was Rs. 520 per share. Sold all shares at Rs. 780 per share on 10-Oct-2025. STT paid on both purchase and sale. Transaction 2 - Unlisted Shares of StartUp Pvt Ltd: Purchased 5,000 shares at Rs. 30 per share on 01-Apr-2023. Sold all shares at Rs. 85 per share on 15-Dec-2025. Transaction 3 - Sovereign Gold Bonds (SGBs): Purchased 50 units at Rs. 4,800 per gram on 01-Mar-2020 (8-year maturity). Sold 20 units on NSE at Rs. 7,200 per gram on 20-Sep-2025. Interest received during FY 2025-26: Rs. 12,000. Transaction 4 - Listed Corporate Bonds: Purchased 500 bonds at Rs. 1,000 face value on 01-Jan-2025. Sold all at Rs. 1,050 on 15-Aug-2025. Annual coupon of 8% received. Transaction 5 - Bonus Shares of InfoSys Ltd: Meera held 1,000 shares of InfoSys purchased on 01-Mar-2022 at Rs. 1,500 per share. She received 1,000 bonus shares (1:1 ratio) on 15-Jun-2023. She sold all 1,000 bonus shares at Rs. 1,800 per share on 20-Jan-2026. STT paid. Meera's total salary income is Rs. 25,00,000 (30% tax bracket). She has no other income. Question 5 of 5: What is the Long-Term Capital Gain on the sale of InfoSys bonus shares, and what is the tax payable on this gain under section 112A, assuming Meera has already utilized the Rs. 1,25,000 exemption against TechCorp LTCG?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** For bonus shares, the cost of acquisition is NIL (Rs. 0) as per the Income Tax Act. The period of holding is counted from the date of allotment of bonus shares, i.e., 15-Jun-2023. Sale date is 20-Jan-2026. Holding period = approximately 31 months, which exceeds 12 months — hence LTCG. Since InfoSys is listed on a recognized stock exchange and STT is paid, section 112A applies. LTCG = (1,800 - 0) x 1,000 = Rs. 18,00,000. The Rs. 1,25,000 exemption under section 112A is an aggregate annual limit across ALL section 112A gains. Since the question states Meera has already utilized this exemption against TechCorp LTCG (Rs. 5,20,000), no further exemption is available for the bonus share LTCG. Tax = 12.5% x Rs. 18,00,000 = Rs. 2,25,000. **The Trap:** Option B uses Rs. 1,500 (original share cost) as the cost of bonus shares and computes gain as (1,800 - 1,500) x 1,000 = 3,00,000. This is wrong — the cost of BONUS shares is always NIL; the original shares retain their own cost basis. Option C applies a fresh Rs. 1,25,000 exemption again on bonus LTCG — the exemption is a single aggregate limit per year, not per transaction. Option D divides the original cost Rs. 1,500 equally between original and bonus shares (750 each), computing gain as (1,800 - 900) x 1,000. This averaging method is incorrect. **Cross-Exam Context:** Bonus share cost = NIL is a high-weightage concept. Also remember: (1) Holding period of bonus shares starts from date of allotment, NOT from date of purchase of original shares. (2) The Rs. 1,25,000 exemption under section 112A is an aggregate annual limit — it cannot be claimed separately for each transaction. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Bonus shares create a significant tax event when sold because the entire sale proceeds (minus NIL cost) become capital gains. Advise clients who receive bonus shares to plan their sale timing carefully. Since the cost is zero, even a small sale price creates a large LTCG. Staggering sales across financial years can help utilize the Rs. 1,25,000 annual exemption multiple times.
